metadata.summary: A convention for authoring methodology content as semantic XML — not as XML for formatting, but as a tag vocabulary where each tag carries meaning the agent can act on. Replaces free-form Markdown ...

content.title: Semantic XML Content for Agents
content.summary: A convention for authoring methodology content as semantic XML — not as XML for formatting, but as a tag vocabulary where each tag carries meaning the agent can act on. Replaces free-form Markdown ...

why.p: Anthropic recommends XML as Claude's preferred input: tags reduce misinterpretation, separate instructions from data, allow different policies per type.

content.summary: Why XML beats Markdown for agent-readable content, and what makes a tag semantic.

section.name: Semantic vs formatting tags
section.p: A tag is semantic when its name describes what the content IS, not how it should look. Markdown only encodes structure (heading, list, paragraph). Semantic XML encodes role: this is a rule, that is an antipattern, this is a reference to an external file.

rule.statement: Every tag name must answer the question "what kind of content does this hold?" — never "how should this be displayed?".
rule.rationale: Anthropic fine-tuned Claude to recognize role-bearing tags (instructions, context, example, thinking). Formatting tags carry no policy signal and waste tokens.

example.code: <rule><statement>Always validate inputs at boundaries.</statement></rule> <antipattern><description>Skipping validation in trusted callers.</description></antipattern>
example.note: Tag names match content roles. Agent can apply different policies per role.

example.code: <bold>Always validate inputs at boundaries.</bold> <paragraph>Skipping validation is risky.</paragraph>
example.note: Formatting tags. Agent learns nothing about the role of each block.

section.name: What semantic XML gives the agent

list.item: Unambiguous separation of instructions, data, examples, and references.
Closed grammar — easier to validate with a schema or parser.
Per-tag policies: agent treats `<rule>` as binding, `<example>` as illustrative, `<antipattern>` as forbidden.
Cheaper future migrations — content is already structured if the schema evolves.

section.name: The four guarantees

rule.statement: A semantic XML document satisfies four guarantees: role-tagged content, closed vocabulary, machine parseability, no presentation logic.
rule.rationale: If any of the four breaks, the document degrades into either Markdown-with-angle-brackets or to free-form prose, losing the policy signal that justified XML in the first place.

section.reference: Closed list of tags allowed in faion-network methodology content.

see-also.link: How to choose tag names and structure attributes.

content.summary: Concrete rules for choosing tag names, when to use attributes, and how to nest hierarchies.

rule.statement: Tag names use lowercase ASCII, hyphenated for multi-word concepts, and describe content role.
rule.rationale: Consistent casing avoids parser ambiguity. Descriptive names let the agent route policy by tag without reading content.

example.note: CamelCase, generic names, or numbered tags carry no role signal.

rule.statement: Use attributes for fixed metadata (id, type, lang, path); use child elements for content the agent will read or quote.
rule.rationale: Attributes are cheap to query and rare to misparse; child text is what the agent renders or quotes back.

example.note: Metadata wrapped as elements bloats the tree and hides what the agent should read.

rule.statement: Nest only when the relationship is naturally hierarchical: container/items, parent-rule/sub-clauses, example/code+note.
rule.rationale: Anthropic's own pattern: documents → document → document_content + source. Flat lists with descriptive tags beat deep trees.

example.note: Five levels of generic wrappers. Agent sees noise.

section.name: Code blocks

rule.statement: Wrap source code in `<code lang="...">` with a CDATA section. Never let `<`, `>`, or `&` leak into element content.
rule.rationale: CDATA preserves code verbatim and prevents the parser from misreading code as XML. The lang attribute lets the agent pick the right interpretation (syntax, formatter, runner).

section.reference: Skeleton with a CDATA-wrapped code example.

rule.statement: Link to other texts and templates with `<reference path="...">` (one-line purpose) or `<see-also><link path="...">...</link></see-also>` (related reading).
rule.rationale: Two distinct relations: a reference is consumed (template, script, schema); a see-also is optional context. Different tags let the agent prioritize.

content.summary: Concrete failure modes when authoring agent-readable XML, with reasons and fixes.

antipattern.reason: Carries no role information. Agent gains nothing over Markdown.

rule.statement: If a tag's purpose is visual emphasis or layout, delete it.

antipattern.description: Mixing `<rule>` in one file and `<policy>` or `<requirement>` in another for the same concept.
antipattern.reason: Agents can apply policy by tag only when the vocabulary is closed and consistent.

rule.statement: Pick a name from the tag glossary. If you need a new tag, propose it as a glossary update first.
rule.rationale: Closed grammars are validatable; ad-hoc grammars drift.

antipattern.description: Wrapping every paragraph in `<text>`, `<content>`, `<block>`, or `<item>` when no role distinction exists.
antipattern.reason: Adds depth without information; hurts both human readers and agent token economy.

antipattern.description: Embedding angle brackets or ampersands in element text without CDATA or entity escaping.
antipattern.reason: Parser breaks. Agent sees corrupted content or refuses to load the file.

example.note: Bare `<` inside element text is a parse error.

example.code: <note>Use < for less-than checks, or wrap code in <code lang="..."> with CDATA.</note>

antipattern.description:
One giant `

` containing a rule, a rationale, an example, and three see-also links.
antipattern.reason: Defeats the point of XML. Split into typed siblings: `<rule>`, `<example>`, `<see-also>`.

section.name: Treating XML as a document format

antipattern.description: Indenting for aesthetics, adding empty lines between every tag, embedding ASCII-art borders.
antipattern.reason: Agent does not care about layout. Whitespace is noise; ASCII art is forbidden by repo rules anyway.

rule.statement: Indent for parser hygiene only (2 spaces per level). Do not pad with blank lines or decorative comments.
